request.question: Are Solo and La Croix Sparkling Water produced in the same country ?

title: Col de la Croix Fry
Highland Spring
Solo (Norwegian soft drink)
La Croix Fry
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome
La Croix Sparkling Water
Alain Hertoghe
Les Sept Paroles du Christ sur la Croix
Col du Glandon
La Croix
sentences: The Col de la Croix Fry (1467 m ) is a mountain pass located in the Chaîne des Aravis, between Manigod and La Clusaz in the Haute-Savoie department of France.
The road over the col is used occasionally by the Tour de France cycle race with the tour crossing the pass on Stage 19 of the 2013 Tour.
At the summit is the village of La Croix Fry.
Highland Spring is a Scottish supplier of bottled water.
It produces still and sparkling water at its factory in Blackford, Perth and Kinross, although despite the name this area is not actually within the Scottish Highlands.
Highland Spring was the highest-selling sparkling water in the UK in 2008 and consolidated its first place position in the UK still water market.
Solo is an orange-flavoured soft drink, owned by the Norwegian companies Ringnes, Oskar Sylte, Aass, and Mack.
The recipe was originally Spanish, and brought to the Tønsberg Bryggeri by Torleif Gulliksrud in 1934.
Solo quickly became Norway's most popular soft drink, and until the 1960s was bigger than Coca-Cola in Norway.
In 1999, Pepsi passed Solo in market share, leaving Solo as third most popular.
La Croix Fry is a small village contained within the Domaine de Manigod.
With a population of under 600 people La Croix Fry is a popular site for hill walking on the Plateau de Beauregard as well as skiing in the winter.
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome, (TC LID: CJU9) , is located on Lac La Croix, Ontario, Canada.

LaCroix or La Croix ( ) is a soda water distributed by the Sundance Beverage Company, a subsidiary of National Beverage Corp. Originating from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin, LaCroix was first produced in 1981.
In the United States, often appealing to sparkling water consumers looking for an alternative to an import, LaCroix markets itself as an "innocent" and "all occasion" beverage.
Alain Hertoghe (born 1959) is a Belgian journalist, formerly an employee of the French Catholic newspaper "La Croix".
He was fired in December 2003 after writing a book critical of the coverage of the U.S. invasion of Iraq by French newspapers "Le Monde", "Le Figaro", "Libération", "Ouest-France" and "La Croix".

Les Sept Paroles du Christ sur la Croix (composed 1859) is a musical setting of The Seven Last Words of Christ by César Franck, though the name in French often refers to an equally well or better known homonymous work by Charles Gounod "Les sept paroles de N.S. Jesus-Christ sur la croix".
Col du Glandon (1924 m ) is a high mountain pass in the Dauphiné Alps in Savoie, France, linking Le Bourg-d'Oisans to La Chambre.
It is situated between the Belledonne, Grandes Rousses and Arvan-Villards mountain ranges, west of the Col de la Croix de Fer.
The road over the Col du Glandon was opened in 1898, although it was not linked to the Col de la Croix de Fer until 1912.
La Croix (] ; English: "The Cross") is a daily French general-interest Roman Catholic newspaper.
It is published in Paris and distributed throughout France, with a circulation of just under 110,000 as of 2009.
It is not explicitly left or right on major political issues, rather adopting the Church's position.
However, "La Croix" ought not be confused with a religious newspaper—its topics are of general interest: world news, the economy, religion and spirituality, parenting, culture and science.
It vigorously advocated for traditional Catholicism while at the same time innovating with the most modern technology and distribution systems.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

hop1_docs: La Croix Sparkling Water | LaCroix or La Croix ( ) is a soda water distributed by the Sundance Beverage Company, a subsidiary of National Beverage Corp. Originating from G. Heileman Brewing Company in La Crosse, Wisconsin, LaCroix was first produced in 1981.  In the United States, often appealing to sparkling water consumers looking for an alternative to an import, LaCroix markets itself as an "innocent" and "all occasion" beverage.
Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome | Lac La Croix Water Aerodrome, (TC LID: CJU9) , is located on Lac La Croix, Ontario, Canada.

Highland Spring | Highland Spring is a Scottish supplier of bottled water.  It produces still and sparkling water at its factory in Blackford, Perth and Kinross, although despite the name this area is not actually within the Scottish Highlands.  Highland Spring was the highest-selling sparkling water in the UK in 2008 and consolidated its first place position in the UK still water market.
Tanja La Croix | Tanja La Croix (born as Tanja Wettach on April 8, 1982) is a House DJ and Producer from Switzerland.

Cava (Spanish wine) | Cava (] , plural "caves") is a sparkling wine of "Denominación de Origen" (DO) status from Catalonia, also produced in other regions of Spain.  It may be white ("blanc") or rosé ("rosat").  The macabeu, parellada and xarel·lo are the most popular and traditional grape varieties for producing cava.  Only wines produced in the champenoise traditional method may be labelled "cava"; those produced by other processes may only be called "sparkling wines" ("vinos espumosos").  About 95% of all cava is produced in the Penedès area in Catalonia, with the village of Sant Sadurní d'Anoia being home to many of Spain's largest production houses.  The two major producers are Codorníu and Freixenet.  Cava is also produced in other villages in Aragon, Castile and León, Extremadura, La Rioja, Basque Country, Navarre and Valencia.

Coteaux Champenois AOC | Coteaux Champenois is a wine Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée (AOC) in the Champagne province of France.  It covers the same area as sparkling Champagne production, but covers only still wines.  The grapes are the same as those allowed for sparkling Champagne: Pinot noir and Pinot Meunier for red wines, and Chardonnay for whites.  Rosé wines are made very rarely, but there is also a rosé AOC in the Champagne region, Rosé des Riceys.  Like sparkling Champagne, most wines are non-vintage.  Production is small, especially in vintages where yields are low, given the high demand for Champagne and the higher profit of producing sparkling wine.

La Croix | La Croix (] ; English: "The Cross") is a daily French general-interest Roman Catholic newspaper.  It is published in Paris and distributed throughout France, with a circulation of just under 110,000 as of 2009.  It is not explicitly left or right on major political issues, rather adopting the Church's position.  However, "La Croix" ought not be confused with a religious newspaper—its topics are of general interest: world news, the economy, religion and spirituality, parenting, culture and science.  It vigorously advocated for traditional Catholicism while at the same time innovating with the most modern technology and distribution systems.

- Fact: [1] La Croix is "distributed by the Sundance Beverage Company, a subsidiary of National Beverage Corp." (manufacturer/distributor info).
- Missing/ambiguous bridge entity: "Solo" — no passage mentions Solo; the name may refer to multiple soft-drink brands (ambiguity/missing).
- Next-hop retrieval (prioritized):
  1. Fetch "Solo (disambiguation)" page (if exists) and/or each candidate page titled "Solo (soft drink)", "Solo (Norwegian soft drink)", "Solo (Australian soft drink)". Extract the lead sentence, infobox fields "Country/Origin" and "Manufacturer/Produced by" (exactly: country of origin/production and producer).
  2. If a single Solo brand page is found, fetch its lead/infobox and the "History" or "Production" section for explicit "produced in" or "originating" statements.
  3. (Optional backup) Re-fetch the LaCroix page lead/infobox to extract an explicit "Country" or "Produced in" field for formal confirmation (page: "La Croix (brand)" — lead/infobox).
- Do not answer final yes/no yet — Solo's production country is not present or disambiguated in the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: Solo disambiguation page country of origin
hop2_docs: The Black Page | "The Black Page #1" is a piece by American composer Frank Zappa known for being extraordinarily difficult to play.  Originally written for the drum kit and melodic percussion (as "The Black Page Drum Solo"), the piece was later rearranged in several versions, including the disco "easy teenage New York version" (commonly referred to as "The Black Page #2") and a so-called "new-age version", among others.

Greg Page (musician) | Gregory John Page, AM (born 16 January 1972) is an Australian singer, musician, and actor.  He is best known as the original lead singer and a founding member of the children's band The Wiggles from 1991 to 2006 and then again from 2012 to 2013.  Page has also recorded several solo albums.

Page One (Steven Page album) | Page One is an album by Steven Page.  It is his third full-length release outside of Barenaked Ladies, and both his second album as the featured performer (after "The Vanity Project"), and his second album for which he was billed in his own name (after "A Singer Must Die" by the Art of Time Ensemble with Steven Page).  It is his first solo album of original material since his departure from Barenaked Ladies in February 2009.

Grammy Award for Best Country Solo Performance | The Grammy Award for Best Country Solo Performance is an award presented at the Grammy Awards, a ceremony that was established in 1958 and originally called the Gramophone Awards.  According to the 54th Grammy Awards description guide it is designed for solo (vocal or instrumental) country recordings and is limited to singles or tracks only.
.codes (disambiguation) | This page is a disambiguation page for . codes (disambiguation).  For the internet generic level domain article; go to . codes.
List of popes by country | This page is a list of popes by country of origin.  They are listed in chronological order within each section.

Hellblazer: Pandemonium | Hellblazer: Pandemonium is an original graphic novel released February 10, 2010 by Vertigo.  The book is intended to mark the 25th anniversary of the first appearance of John Constantine in the pages of "Swamp Thing" and was written by Jamie Delano, the original writer for the character's solo series "Hellblazer", with art by Jock.

The retrieved passages contain no explicit information identifying which "Solo" soft-drink is intended or stating Solo's country of production.
response.answer: Cannot determine: La Croix is produced in the United States, but the summaries give no information on which Solo is intended or where Solo is produced, so it is unknown whether they are produced in the same country.
